ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised beef brisket noodles; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Hong Shao Niu Nan Mian; A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.

main_ingredients: wheat noodles (egg noodles or flat rice noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef brisket noodles weighs approximately 500 g and contains roughly 650 kcal, making it a complete and filling single-meal serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient composition of mixed Chinese noodle dishes and braised beef — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Hong Shao Niu Nan Mian as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised beef brisket (USDA FDC ID 174032) and cooked egg noodles (USDA FDC ID 172030), combined with HPB Singapore data for similar mixed noodle dishes. Input per_100g values from the enriched_json pipeline are consistent with this composite and have been retained where they align with approved-source ranges. Sodium value (360 mg/100 g) is consistent with HPB data for soy-sauce-based noodle dishes. All values cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — noodle dishes with protein and fat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hong Shao Niu Nan Mian. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous wheat noodle dishes with substantial protein and fat content from the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., beef noodle soup analogues, GI ~40–50). The high protein (9 g/100 g) and fat (5.6 g/100 g) content from slow-braised beef, together with the gelatinous collagen matrix of the brisket, are well-established factors that slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response relative to plain noodles (GI ~55–65). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 55.0) / 100 = 24.8 (500 g serving × 11.0 g carbohydrate/100 g = 55.0 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (360 mg/100 g; ~1800 mg per 500 g bowl) from soy sauce, chu hou paste, and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid consuming the full braising gravy, and request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparations; a single bowl can contribute up to 78% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (11 g/100 g; ~55 g per bowl) with low estimated GI (~45) due to protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions; the low GI (~45) and high protein content support glycemic control, but the total carbohydrate load per bowl (~55 g) requires accounting within daily carbohydrate targets, and the high sodium content warrants caution if hypertension co-exists.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (360 mg/100 g), saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g), and cholesterol (24 mg/100 g) from beef brisket and braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency due to the high sodium and saturated fat content; choosing a smaller portion (300 g), discarding excess gravy, and pairing with additional blanched vegetables can reduce the cardiovascular risk profile of this meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Slow-cooked collagen from beef brisket connective tissue; anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, star anise)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The slow-cooked collagen and anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, star anise) may offer modest benefit; however, high red meat intake should be balanced with anti-inflammatory vegetables, and the high sodium content should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket Noodles (红烧牛腩面 / 紅燒牛腩麵, Hóngshāo niúnǎn miàn) is a quintessential Cantonese comfort dish originating from Guangdong province and deeply embedded in Hong Kong food culture. The dish centres on beef brisket — a collagen-rich cut — that is slow-braised for two to four hours in a master stock of dark and light soy sauce, chu hou paste (a fermented bean paste), oyster sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, and dried tangerine peel. This prolonged braising renders the brisket meltingly tender and gelatinous, while the cooking liquid reduces into a thick, intensely savoury, dark-brown gravy. The braised beef and its gravy are ladled over a portion of freshly cooked wheat noodles — typically thin egg noodles (细蛋面) or flat rice noodles (河粉) — and the bowl is completed with blanched leafy greens such as choy sum (菜心) or bok choy (白菜). Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 130 kcal, 9 g protein, 5.6 g fat (2.4 g saturated), and 11 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 360 mg reflecting the soy-sauce and paste-heavy braising liquid. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45) due to the moderating effect of protein and fat on carbohydrate digestion. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and therefore a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management; portion control and gravy reduction are the primary mitigation strategies. The dish is a good source of zinc, vitamin B12, niacin, and iron from the beef component.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers Hong Shao Niu Nan Mian as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised beef brisket and cooked egg noodles, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for similar mixed noodle dishes. The input pipeline values are consistent with these approved-source ranges and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite braised beef noodle dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g, consistent with trace levels expected from ruminant-derived beef fat (naturally occurring vaccenic/conjugated linoleic acid isomers); this is a plausible and defensible value. 4. Portions: the input contained two duplicate entries for '1 serving (450g)'; the canonical default serving has been set to 500 g (1 bowl) per the grabfood-sourced portion, which is the more commonly cited restaurant serving size. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (45 × 55.0) / 100 = 24.8, where 55.0 g is the carbohydrate content of a 500 g bowl (500 g × 11.0 g/100 g). 6. The input source cited 'Hong Kong Food and Environmental Hygiene Department' (CFS); this organization is not on the approved source list. Nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore as the approved primary sources. The CFS reference has been removed from the authoritative nutrient_source field. 7. Halal status is marked false as chu hou paste and soy sauce preparations may contain non-halal ingredients; this should be verified for specific restaurant preparations.
